# Dynamic Slug Blog with RedwoodSDK

## Background

Build a small server-rendered Markdown blog using RedwoodSDK (rwsdk). The blog reads its posts from a JSON file, renders Markdown to HTML on the server, exposes a raw markdown endpoint, and returns a 404 for unknown slugs.

## Requirements

* An index page at `/` that lists all posts as cards. Each card must show the post title, author, published date, and a link to the post detail page.
* A detail page at `/posts/:slug` that renders the post's Markdown body to HTML. The HTML document for the detail page must:
  * Set the document `<title>` to the post title.
  * Include an `<h1>` whose text matches the post title.
  * Include a `<meta name="description">` tag whose `content` is the first 100 characters of the post's raw markdown body, HTML-escaped.
* A raw endpoint at `/posts/:slug/raw` that returns the raw markdown body as `text/plain`.
* Requests to unknown slugs (`/posts/:slug` or `/posts/:slug/raw`) must return HTTP status `404`.
* Posts must be loaded from `src/data/posts.json` and seeded with exactly the three posts described below.

## Implementation Hints

* The project has been scaffolded with `npx create-rwsdk@latest` already; you only need to add routes, the `posts.json` file, and the Markdown rendering logic.
* Use rwsdk's dynamic path parameters (e.g. `route("/posts/:slug", ...)`) — `params.slug` is exposed on the request handler.
* Use a Markdown library such as `marked` or `markdown-it` (already installed) to convert Markdown to HTML on the server.
* HTML-escape the meta-description content so quotes and angle brackets do not break the markup.
* For unknown slugs, return a `new Response(..., { status: 404 })`.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: npm run dev
* Port: 5173
* Posts data file: `src/data/posts.json` must exist and contain the three seeded posts (slugs: `hello-rwsdk`, `cf-edge-tips`, `react-rsc-101`).
* Routes:
  * `GET /`: Returns status `200`, `Content-Type: text/html`, and lists each post with its title, author, and published date. Each card must contain an anchor `<a href="/posts/<slug>">` linking to the corresponding detail page.
  * `GET /posts/:slug` (known slug): Returns status `200`, `Content-Type: text/html`. The HTML must contain:
    * A `<title>` element whose text equals the post title.
    * An `<h1>` whose text equals the post title.
    * A `<meta name="description" content="...">` whose content is the first 100 characters of the post's raw markdown body, HTML-escaped.
    * The Markdown body rendered as HTML.
  * `GET /posts/:slug/raw` (known slug): Returns status `200`, `Content-Type` starting with `text/plain`, and a body that exactly matches the raw markdown stored in `posts.json`.
  * `GET /posts/unknown-slug` and `GET /posts/unknown-slug/raw`: Return status `404`.
Let me start by exploring the project structure to understand what's already been scaffolded.

  children,
}) => (

        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />

    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

[
  {
    "slug": "hello-rwsdk",
    "title": "Hello RedwoodSDK",
    "author": "Alice Redwood",
    "publishedAt": "2024-01-15",
    "body": "# Hello RedwoodSDK

Welcome to **RedwoodSDK** — the full-stack framework built for Cloudflare Workers.

## Getting Started

RedwoodSDK makes it easy to build server-rendered React applications that run at the edge.

- Fast cold-start times
- Built-in routing
- React Server Components support

Give it a try and see how quickly you can ship!
"
  },
  {
    "slug": "cf-edge-tips",
    "title": "Cloudflare Edge Tips",
    "author": "Bob Cloudman",
    "publishedAt": "2024-02-20",
    "body": "# Cloudflare Edge Tips

Running code at the **edge** means your users get faster responses no matter where they are in the world.

## Top Tips

1. Use **KV** for low-latency reads of configuration and content.
2. Leverage **Durable Objects** for coordinating stateful workloads.
3. Keep your worker bundles small — every byte counts at startup.
4. Cache aggressively with `Cache-Control` headers.

> The edge is not a magic bullet, but it is a very sharp one.
"
  },
  {
    "slug": "react-rsc-101",
    "title": "React Server Components 101",
    "author": "Carol Components",
    "publishedAt": "2024-03-10",
    "body": "# React Server Components 101

React Server Components (RSC) let you render components **on the server** without sending their JavaScript to the client.

## Why RSC?

- Smaller client bundles
- Direct access to server-side data sources
- Improved Time to First Byte (TTFB)

## How It Works

Server components are rendered once on the server and streamed to the client as a special React payload. Client components hydrate in the browser as usual.

Combined with RedwoodSDK, RSC is a powerful tool for building fast, data-driven apps.
"
  }
]

part.text: Now let me check how rwsdk route handlers work to understand the request/params API:

output.error: EISDIR: illegal operation on a directory, read

part.text: Now I have a complete understanding of the rwsdk API. Let me also check the `DocumentProps` type:

Now I understand the full picture. The `Document` component receives `DocumentProps` which extends `RequestInfo` (includes `params`, `path`, etc.) plus `children`. I can use a different approach for the detail page — instead of passing props through Document, I'll have the post detail page render a complete HTML response directly (no Document wrapper needed for the raw endpoint), and for the HTML page I can use a separate Document that accepts title/description.

Let me think about the cleanest approach:

1. For the index page (`/`): uses the existing `render(Document, [...])` pattern.
2. For `/posts/:slug`: needs a custom Document with dynamic title/meta. I can pass the title/description as part of the route by creating a custom wrapper.
3. For `/posts/:slug/raw`: returns a plain `Response` directly from the route handler.

The cleanest approach for the detail page is to have the route handler return a full `Response` with the HTML (bypassing the Document/render system), or use a custom Document. Looking at the DocumentProps type, the Document receives the full RequestInfo as props, so I can access `params` inside Document. However, the title/description need to be computed from the post data.

The best approach: for the detail page, return a `Response` directly from the route handler with the full HTML content. This avoids the Document wrapping complexity entirely for dynamic title/meta.

Now let me implement everything:

part.text: The 100 char slice contains newlines which are valid in HTML attribute values (they get normalized by browsers). However, to be clean, let me check if the acceptance criteria requires the content to be a single line or just HTML-escaped. The spec says "HTML-escaped" — newlines in attribute values are technically fine in HTML5. But to be safe and standards-compliant, I should escape them too. Let me update the `htmlEscape` function to also replace newlines:

All requirements are fully satisfied. Here's a summary of what was built:

input.result:
The Markdown blog has been implemented. Here's what was created/modified:

### Files Created
- **`src/data/posts.json`** — Three seeded posts with slugs `hello-rwsdk`, `cf-edge-tips`, and `react-rsc-101`, each with `slug`, `title`, `author`, `publishedAt`, and `body` fields.
- **`src/app/pages/post.ts`** — Two route handlers:
  - `postDetailHandler` — Finds the post by slug, renders Markdown to HTML via `marked`, builds a full HTML document with `<title>`, `<h1>`, and `<meta name="description" content="...">` (first 100 chars of raw body, HTML-escaped with newlines normalized). Returns 404 for unknown slugs.
  - `postRawHandler` — Returns the raw markdown body as `text/plain`. Returns 404 for unknown slugs.

### Files Modified
- **`src/app/pages/home.tsx`** — Replaced the Welcome placeholder with a posts listing. Each post renders as a card showing title (linked to `/posts/:slug`), author, and published date.
- **`src/worker.tsx`** — Added two new routes before the `render(Document, [...])` block:
  - `route("/posts/:slug/raw", postRawHandler)` — registered first to prevent `:slug` from absorbing the `/raw` suffix
  - `route("/posts/:slug", postDetailHandler)`

### All acceptance criteria verified:
| Route | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `GET /` | 200 | Lists all 3 posts with title, author, date, and `<a href="/posts/:slug">` links |
| `GET /posts/hello-rwsdk` | 200 | `text/html`, has `<title>`, `<h1>`, `<meta name="description">`, rendered Markdown |
| `GET /posts/cf-edge-tips` | 200 | Same structure |
| `GET /posts/react-rsc-101` | 200 | Same structure |
| `GET /posts/hello-rwsdk/raw` | 200 | `text/plain`, exact raw markdown body |
| `GET /posts/unknown-slug` | 404 | |
| `GET /posts/unknown-slug/raw` | 404 | |
